description: Release-polish: fix two worklist annoyances surfaced during the codex enforcement work.

items.before: After clicking Approve/Drop, the Workspace component dims (opacity 0.3) until the worklist DataSource onLoaded handler detects a content change or 60-second stale timeout, then clears the inflight flag and undims. The content-change clear is too eager: if the agent's first worklist write transitions items proposed→applied (mid-turn, still working toward a commit and prune), the items signature differs from the stored snapshot, clearInflight fires, the surface undims even though the agent is still processing the same approval. Same problem on approve-to-commit: agent prunes the item after committing, signature differs, undim happens before the agent has finished wrapping up the turn (push prompt, summary, etc.). Considered (a) keeping content-change clear but increasing the stale timeout to compensate — rejected, doesn't solve the mid-turn case; (b) tracking agent turn boundaries via the session JSONL the way Transcript does — rejected for v1, adds a polling source and couples Workspace to session-reader internals; (c) only clear on stale-timeout (60s) or explicit Cancel — chosen, simplest fix matching user intent ('remain dimmed when in flight').
items.after: Remove the content-comparison branch from the worklist DataSource's onLoaded handler. Keep two clear paths: (1) the stale timeout (bumped to 120s since we no longer rely on content change to clear faster — gives slow agent turns room without leaving stale dimming forever); (2) the Cancel pending button which the user can click any time. The localStorage flag still seeds `submitting` on mount via `var.submitting="{!!getInflight()}"`, so a closed/reopened drawer still picks up the dim state. The onLoaded handler keeps its prune-checked-ids housekeeping but drops the inflight-clear logic. Trade-off: in the clarifying-question case (agent responds without touching the worklist), dimming now persists until the 120s timeout or until the user clicks Cancel — acceptable because the user can always recover with the button, and over-dimming is less confusing than under-dimming.
